skill.name: docx-official
skill.description: Create, inspect, edit, and validate Microsoft Word DOCX files using OOXML workflows. Supports text extraction, raw XML access, comments, tracked changes, redlining, packing, and visual conversion.
skill.summary: Work with DOCX files through OOXML, tracked changes, comments, validation, and document conversion workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many hits, but most Markdown, namespace URL, C2 keyword, and weak-crypto findings are false positives from documentation and OOXML schemas. The confirmed issue is unsafe ZIP extraction in document helpers, which can allow Zip Slip writes when processing crafted Office files. No evidence found of malicious intent or prompt injection, so this is high risk rather than critical.

high_findings.title: Unsafe Archive Extraction in Office Helpers
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: Office files are ZIP archives, and multiple helpers call extractall on user-provided documents without validating member paths. A crafted DOCX, PPTX, or XLSX archive could write files outside the intended temporary or output directory.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact vulnerable pattern is present on archive inputs controlled by document files. Python zipfile.extractall does not enforce a safe destination boundary for malicious member names.

medium_findings.title: Privileged and Global Dependency Installation Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs agents to install packages with sudo apt-get, npm install -g, and pip install. This is legitimate setup guidance, but it can modify the host system and fetch code from package repositories during a document task.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and include privileged or global package installation. The context is dependency setup, so this is risky operational guidance rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: External Tool Execution on User Documents
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: Helper scripts call external programs such as soffice and git using fixed argument arrays. I did not find shell=True or direct command injection, but external parsers will process user-supplied document content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Subprocess usage is confirmed, but arguments are hardcoded lists and temporary file paths. Residual risk comes from invoking complex external tools on untrusted documents.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Many external command findings in SKILL.md, docx-js.md, and ooxml.md are Markdown code spans or fenced examples. They document workflows and are not Ruby backtick execution inside runnable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are Markdown documentation, and the backticks delimit inline commands or code blocks. No executable Ruby context exists at these locations.

low_findings.title: OOXML Schema Terms Misclassified as Malware Indicators
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static hits for C2 keywords, weak cryptography, network tools, and path traversal mostly come from OOXML schema names, namespace URLs, enumerations, and schemaLocation values. I did not find evidence that these schema files execute code or initiate network activity.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled findings are XML schema definitions and vocabulary values. They are data files used for validation, not executable logic.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Namespaces or License References
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Hardcoded URL findings are primarily XML namespace identifiers, schema references, or license terms. No evidence found that these URLs are contacted by the skill at runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are static identifiers or legal references in the inspected context. I did not find HTTP client code using these values.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unsafe Archive Extraction in Office Helpers
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: Office files are ZIP archives, and multiple helpers call extractall on user-provided documents without validating member paths. A crafted DOCX, PPTX, or XLSX archive could write files outside the intended temporary or output directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact vulnerable pattern is present on archive inputs controlled by document files. Python zipfile.extractall does not enforce a safe destination boundary for malicious member names.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged and Global Dependency Installation Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs agents to install packages with sudo apt-get, npm install -g, and pip install. This is legitimate setup guidance, but it can modify the host system and fetch code from package repositories during a document task.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and include privileged or global package installation. The context is dependency setup, so this is risky operational guidance rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Tool Execution on User Documents
dangerous_patterns.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: Helper scripts call external programs such as soffice and git using fixed argument arrays. I did not find shell=True or direct command injection, but external parsers will process user-supplied document content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Subprocess usage is confirmed, but arguments are hardcoded lists and temporary file paths. Residual risk comes from invoking complex external tools on untrusted documents.

content.user_title: Edit Word Documents with OOXML Workflows
content.value_statement: Professional Word edits require preserving structure, comments, and tracked changes. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code DOCX workflows for inspection, redlining, and validation.
actual_capabilities: Create new Word documents with docx-js examples and packaging guidance.
Unpack DOCX, PPTX, and XLSX files for raw OOXML inspection and editing.
Edit Word comments, replies, tracked insertions, and tracked deletions through helper libraries.
Convert DOCX content to Markdown for review, including tracked change views.
Validate edited documents against OOXML schema and redlining rules.
Convert Word documents to PDF and page images for visual inspection.
limitations: Requires external tools such as pandoc, LibreOffice, Poppler, npm, and Python packages.
Archive extraction helpers need path validation before processing untrusted Office files.
Does not provide a full visual Word editor or guarantee exact rendering parity.
Most advanced automation focuses on Word DOCX, even though some scripts mention PPTX and XLSX.

use_cases.title: Review Legal Redlines
use_cases.description: Plan and apply precise tracked changes while preserving unchanged text, comments, and document structure.

use_cases.title: Prepare Technical Documents
use_cases.description: Create or update structured Word documents with headings, tables, formatting, and verification steps.

use_cases.title: Automate DOCX Workflows
use_cases.description: Inspect OOXML parts, modify document XML, repack files, and validate outputs in repeatable scripts.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Document Text
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of an existing DOCX file
prompt_templates.prompt: Read this DOCX file and summarize its sections. Preserve headings, comments, and any visible tracked changes in the summary.

prompt_templates.title: Create a New Word File
prompt_templates.scenario: Generate a formatted document from requirements
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a DOCX document from this outline. Include title styling, headings, bullet lists, and a short table where useful.

prompt_templates.title: Apply Tracked Changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this DOCX and apply the requested edits as tracked changes. Make minimal precise edits and preserve comments and formatting.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Complex OOXML Edits
prompt_templates.prompt: Unpack this DOCX, inspect the relevant OOXML parts, apply the requested structural changes, repack it, and validate the final document.

output_examples.input: A contract DOCX needs party name corrections and date updates with tracked changes.
output_examples.output: A revised Word document with precise insertions and deletions, plus a short verification summary of each changed clause.

output_examples.input: A report outline needs to become a formatted Word document.
output_examples.output: A DOCX file with styled headings, paragraphs, lists, and tables that match the requested structure.

output_examples.input: A document may have broken comments after manual XML edits.
output_examples.output: A validation report explaining comment, relationship, or schema issues and the document areas that need correction.

best_practices: Work on a copy of the original document and keep the source file unchanged.
Use small batches for tracked changes and validate after each meaningful batch.
Inspect raw OOXML only when markdown extraction is not enough for the task.
anti_patterns: Do not process untrusted Office archives outside a sandbox until extraction paths are validated.
Do not replace whole paragraphs when only a few words need tracked changes.
Do not install global dependencies during a document task without explicit user approval.

faq.question: Can this skill create new DOCX files?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents a docx-js workflow for creating formatted Word documents from JavaScript or TypeScript.

faq.question: Can it preserve tracked changes?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes redlining workflows and patterns for precise insertions and deletions in OOXML.

faq.question: Does it support comments and replies?
faq.answer: Yes. The Python document helper includes methods for adding comments and replying to existing comments.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted DOCX files?
faq.answer: Use caution. The audit found unsafe archive extraction helpers, so untrusted files should be sandboxed first.

faq.question: Which external tools are expected?
faq.answer: The workflows may use pandoc, LibreOffice, Poppler, npm docx, Python, defusedxml, and git.

faq.question: Can it edit PPTX or XLSX files?
faq.answer: Some pack and validation helpers mention PPTX and XLSX, but the main documented workflows focus on Word DOCX.
